section.title: Praise the Lord, O My Soul
section.sectionRationale: The first of the Psalter's closing Hallelujah psalms. It moves from a resolve to praise for life, through a warning against trusting mortal princes, to the LORD's own resume of compassionate justice for the oppressed, hungry, prisoners, blind, and bowed down, the very works Jesus points to as signs of the kingdom, Luke 7:22, Matthew 11:5.
section.thesis: Mortal rulers cannot save and their plans perish with them, but the LORD who made heaven and earth executes justice, feeds the hungry, frees prisoners, opens blind eyes, lifts the bowed down, and reigns forever, works fulfilled in Jesus and consummated when He returns as Zion's eternal King.
section.frameNote: Four movements: a resolve to praise for life, verses 1-2; a warning against trusting mortal princes, verses 3-4; blessedness in the God of Jacob and His compassionate works for the oppressed, verses 5-9; and the LORD's eternal reign over Zion, verse 10.

units.label: Praise the Lord, O my soul
units.note: a resolve to praise for life

verses.text: {{haleluyah|Hallelujah}}! Praise the LORD, O my soul.
I will praise the LORD all my life; I will sing praises to my God while I have my being.

units.label: Put not your trust in princes

verses.text: Put not your trust in princes, in mortal man, who cannot save.
When his spirit departs, he returns to the ground; on that very day his plans perish.

units.label: Blessed is he whose help is the God of Jacob
units.note: the LORD's compassionate justice for the oppressed

verses.text: {{ashrei|Blessed}} is he whose help is the God of Jacob, whose hope is in the LORD his God,
the Maker of heaven and earth, the sea, and everything in them. He remains faithful forever.
He executes justice for the oppressed and gives food to the hungry. The LORD sets the prisoners free,
the LORD opens the eyes of the blind, the LORD lifts those who are weighed down, the LORD loves the righteous.
The LORD protects {{ger|foreigners}}; He sustains the fatherless and the widow, but the ways of the wicked He frustrates.
The LORD reigns forever, your God, O Zion, for all generations. {{haleluyah|Hallelujah}}!

haleluyah.gloss: An imperative call, praise the LORD (Yah, a short form of Yahweh). It opens and closes each of the last five psalms, framing the whole Psalter's final movement as one continuous shout of praise.

ashrei.gloss: Blessed, happy, or fortunate. The same word opens Psalm 1; true blessedness is found not in princes but in the God of Jacob, whose help never fails.

ger.gloss: A foreigner or resident alien living among God's people. The LORD's justice extends beyond Israel to the vulnerable outsider, named here alongside the fatherless and the widow.

basic.context: This psalm opens the five closing Hallelujah psalms of the Psalter, 146 to 150. It moves from a private resolve to praise, to a warning against trusting mortal rulers, to a hymn of the LORD's compassionate justice for the oppressed, hungry, imprisoned, blind, bowed down, and foreign, culminating in His eternal reign over Zion.
basic.hermeneutics: This is a wisdom psalm of trust, contrasting mortal princes, whose plans perish at death, with the eternal God whose faithful works never fail. The abiding call is exclusive trust in the LORD rather than in any human power, however impressive.
basic.originalLanguages: The psalm opens and closes with haleluyah, praise the LORD. Verse 5 pronounces ashrei, blessed, on the one whose help is the God of Jacob. Verse 9 names the ger, the foreigner, among those the LORD protects, widening His justice beyond Israel's own borders to the vulnerable outsider.
basic.typology: The LORD's works in verses 7 to 9, justice for the oppressed, food for the hungry, freedom for prisoners, sight for the blind, lifting for the bowed down, are the very works Jesus names as signs of the kingdom when John's disciples ask if He is the one to come, Luke 7:22 and Matthew 11:5, and the works He reads from Isaiah 61 in the Nazareth synagogue, Luke 4:18-19.
basic.christ: Jesus is the LORD's compassion in the flesh. He reads Isaiah 61 in the synagogue and claims its fulfillment, then later answers John's disciples by pointing to the blind seeing, the lame walking, and the poor hearing good news, the very list this psalm gives as proof of who He is, and of who reigns forever over Zion.
basic.god: God is shown as the only trustworthy helper, eternal and faithful, who acts in justice and compassion for the oppressed, hungry, imprisoned, blind, bowed down, righteous, foreigner, fatherless, and widow, and who reigns forever as Zion's King, in contrast to mortal princes whose plans perish.
basic.crossReferences: Luke 4:18-19 shows Jesus reading Isaiah 61 and claiming the ministry of justice, sight, and freedom this psalm ascribes to the LORD. Luke 7:22 and Matthew 11:5 show Jesus pointing to these same works as proof of His identity. Revelation 11:15 and Daniel 7:14 echo the psalm's confession that the LORD reigns forever.
basic.oneStory: This is the LORD's own resume of compassionate justice, fulfilled when Jesus, God come near, does these very things in flesh and blood, and fulfilled fully when He returns to reign forever as Zion's true King.

context.literary: The first of the Psalter's final five Hallelujah psalms, each opening and closing with the same shout of praise. It falls into two main stanzas, verses 1 to 4 warning against trust in man, and verses 5 to 10 commending trust in God, with a rapid sequence of seven participles in verses 7 to 9 portraying the LORD's active compassion.
context.historical: Likely composed for post-exilic worship, when the fragility of human rulers, Persian governors and local princes alike, would have sharpened the contrast with the LORD's own unfailing reign from Zion.
context.canonical: Sets the tone for the Psalter's final movement, from private resolve to praise, through the LORD's compassionate justice, to universal praise in Psalms 148 to 150. Its list of the LORD's works becomes, in the Gospels, a direct description of Jesus' own ministry.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To turn worshippers away from confidence in mortal rulers, whose plans perish at death, and toward confident, lifelong praise of the LORD, whose faithful, compassionate justice never fails and whose reign never ends.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The psalm describes the LORD's specific acts of justice and compassion; it prescribes as an abiding pattern exclusive trust in God rather than in human power, and active care for the oppressed, the hungry, and the vulnerable outsider.

debates.question: Does verse 3's warning against trusting princes rule out all confidence in human leaders and institutions?

views.label: An absolute warning against all human trust
views.case: No human authority deserves ultimate trust, since only God saves; verse 4 grounds the warning in mortality itself, not in any particular ruler's failure.

views.label: A relative warning against misplaced ultimate trust
views.case: Scripture elsewhere commands honor and obedience to legitimate authority, Romans 13:1-7; the psalm's target is looking to princes as saviors, not the existence of proper human leadership.

debates.bearing: Both readings agree that ultimate trust belongs to God alone; the difference is whether the psalm devalues legitimate human authority or simply relativizes it against the LORD's eternal, unfailing help.

study.originalLanguages: The psalm opens and closes with haleluyah, praise the LORD. Verse 5 pronounces ashrei, blessed, on the one whose help is the God of Jacob, echoing Psalm 1's opening word. Verse 9 names the ger, the foreigner, among those the LORD protects, alongside the fatherless and the widow.

typology.type: The LORD's works for the oppressed, hungry, imprisoned, blind, and bowed down, verses 7-9
typology.fulfillment: Fulfilled in Jesus, who reads this same list from Isaiah 61 as His own mission, Luke 4:18-19, and points to it as proof of His identity, Luke 7:22, Matthew 11:5.

typology.type: The LORD's eternal reign over Zion, verse 10
typology.fulfillment: Fulfilled in Christ's everlasting kingdom, Daniel 7:14, Revelation 11:15, the true and final answer to every mortal prince whose plans perish.

study.christ: Jesus is the LORD's compassion enacted in flesh and blood. He reads Isaiah 61 in the Nazareth synagogue and claims its fulfillment, Luke 4:18-19, and He answers John the Baptist's question about His identity by pointing to the blind seeing, the lame walking, and the poor hearing good news, Luke 7:22, Matthew 11:5, the very works this psalm ascribes to the LORD. He is also the eternal King in whom the LORD's reign over Zion, verse 10, finds its lasting fulfillment.
study.god: God is the only trustworthy helper, unlike mortal princes whose plans perish at death. He is Maker of heaven and earth, faithful forever, and actively just and compassionate toward the oppressed, hungry, imprisoned, blind, bowed down, righteous, foreigner, fatherless, and widow. He reigns forever as Zion's God for all generations.

crossReferences.note: Jesus reads Isaiah 61 in the synagogue, claiming the ministry of justice, sight, and freedom this psalm ascribes to the LORD.
Jesus answers John's disciples by pointing to these same signs as proof of His identity.
Echoes the psalm's confession that the LORD, and His Christ, will reign forever.

study.oneStory: This is the LORD's own resume of compassionate justice, executing justice for the oppressed, feeding the hungry, freeing prisoners, opening blind eyes, lifting the bowed down. It is fulfilled when Jesus, God come near, does these very things in flesh and blood, and it will be fulfilled fully when He returns to reign forever as Zion's true and everlasting King.

quiz.q: What does Psalm 146 warn against trusting for salvation?

options.text: Princes and mortal man, who cannot save, whose plans perish when they die.
options.note: Verses 3-4 ground the warning in human mortality itself.

options.text: Wealth and riches accumulated in this life.
options.note: A related biblical theme, but this psalm's specific warning is against trusting mortal rulers.

options.text: Idols carved by human hands.
options.note: A real biblical concern elsewhere, but not this psalm's specific target.

options.text: Nothing; the psalm teaches self-reliance.
options.note: The psalm teaches trust in God, not in self or in princes.

quiz.q: What works does the LORD do for the oppressed, hungry, prisoners, blind, and bowed down, verses 7-9?

options.text: He executes justice, feeds the hungry, frees prisoners, opens blind eyes, and lifts up the bowed down.
options.note: This sevenfold list of active compassion is the psalm's centerpiece.

options.text: He promises a future reward without acting now.
options.note: The psalm describes present, active works, not merely a future promise.

options.text: He commands the wealthy to help them instead.
options.note: The LORD Himself is the subject of every verb in this passage.

options.text: He ignores them until the final judgment.
options.note: The opposite is true; the LORD acts for them now.

quiz.q: How does Jesus point to Psalm 146's language of God's compassion?

options.text: By pointing to His own healing of the blind, lifting the bowed down, and good news to the poor as proof of His identity, Luke 7:22 and Matthew 11:5.
options.note: Jesus directly echoes this psalm's list when answering John's disciples.

options.text: By quoting Psalm 146 word for word in a parable.
options.note: Jesus alludes to and enacts this list of works rather than quoting the psalm verbatim.

options.text: By rebuking the psalm's hope in worldly deliverance.
options.note: Jesus fulfills, rather than rebukes, this psalm's hope.

options.text: By reserving these works only for the age to come.
options.note: Jesus performs these works in His earthly ministry, though their fullness awaits His return.

quiz.q: Who does the LORD especially protect, according to verse 9?

options.text: The foreigner, the fatherless, and the widow.
options.note: The ger, foreigner, is named alongside the classic biblical pair, the fatherless and the widow.

options.text: Only the native-born Israelite.
options.note: The verse explicitly includes the foreigner, widening the LORD's protection beyond Israel's own.

options.text: Only kings and princes.
options.note: The psalm has just warned against trusting princes; verse 9 names the vulnerable, not the powerful.

options.text: Only those who prove themselves worthy.
options.note: The LORD's protection here is unearned, extended to the vulnerable by His own character.

quiz.q: What is the psalm's climactic confession in verse 10?

options.text: The LORD reigns forever, your God, O Zion, for all generations.
options.note: This closing declaration answers the opening warning against mortal princes whose reigns end.

options.text: Human kings will reign forever.
options.note: The opposite is true; only the LORD's reign is everlasting.

options.text: The world will end in chaos.
options.note: The psalm ends in confident hope, not in chaos.

options.text: God's reign will one day come to an end.
options.note: The verse says the very opposite, that His reign is for all generations.

questions: Where are you tempted to put ultimate trust in a "prince," a leader, a position, a portfolio, or a plan, that cannot ultimately save?
The LORD executes justice for the oppressed and feeds the hungry, verse 7. Where might God be calling you to join His work of compassion this week?
Jesus points to these very works, sight to the blind, freedom to the captive, as signs of His kingdom. How does that shape your hope when your own body or circumstances feel bowed down?
Verse 9 names the foreigner alongside the fatherless and the widow. Who is the vulnerable outsider in your community that this psalm calls you to notice?
The psalm ends, "The LORD reigns forever." What situation in your life needs that reminder right now?

translationNotes.note: Hallelujah renders halelu-yah, an imperative plural, praise Yah; it opens and closes each of the last five psalms of the Psalter.
Spirit renders ruach, which can also mean breath, the same word used of the breath of life in Genesis 2:7, underscoring human frailty apart from God.
Foreigners renders ger, a resident alien; the sense includes any such vulnerable outsider living among God's people.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched via WebSearch and web_fetch from biblehub.com's BSB page for Psalm 146 (https://biblehub.com/bsb/psalms/146.htm) after the local makor-scripture toolkit returned a disk I/O error. Footnote marker [a] stripped. No em or en dashes present in the source verses. No sermon from the trusted cluster (Begg/truthforlife.org, Keller/gospelinlife.com, Piper/desiringgod.org, Ferguson, Carson/thegospelcoalition.org) was found verified for Psalm 146 this session; a Christopher Ash sermon exists on truthforlife.org but is not by Begg, so sermon status is set to none rather than fabricated.
